verses.text: Now these are the divisions of the sons of Aaron. The sons of Aaron; Nadab, and Abihu, Eleazar, and Ithamar. But Nadab and Abihu died before their father, and had no children: therefore Eleazar and Ithamar executed the priest's office. And David distributed them, both Zadok of the sons of Eleazar, and Ahimelech of the sons of Ithamar, according to their offices in their service. And there were more chief men found of the sons of Eleazar than of the sons of Ithamar; and thus were they divided. Among the sons of Eleazar there were sixteen chief men of the house of their fathers, and eight among the sons of Ithamar according to the house of their fathers. Thus were they divided by lot, one sort with another; for the governors of the sanctuary, and governors of the house of God, were of the sons of Eleazar, and of the sons of Ithamar. And Shemaiah the son of Nethaneel the scribe, one of the Levites, wrote them before the king, and the princes, and Zadok the priest, and Ahimelech the son of Abiathar, and before the chief of the fathers of the priests and Levites: one principal household being taken for Eleazar, and one taken for Ithamar. Guidance: David organizes the priests, the sons of Aaron, into divisions for their service. The Chronicler recalls that Aaron's eldest sons, Nadab and Abihu, 'died before their father, and had no children' (struck down for offering strange fire, Lev 10), so the priesthood continued through Eleazar and Ithamar. Their descendants are now divided 'by lot,' fairly, with a scribe recording each assignment before the king and the chief men. The chapter is showing us the orderly arrangement of the priesthood, that the service of God's house might be carried on without confusion or favoritism. The chapter is teaching us, in the sober mention of Nadab and Abihu, that priestly privilege is no protection against the consequences of irreverence; the LORD is holy, and those nearest to Him are held most strictly accountable. Their line was cut off, and the priesthood passed to their brothers — a standing warning that nearness to holy things demands holy fear. The chapter is teaching us the wisdom of dividing the work 'by lot,' impartially: where one family had more chief men than another, the lot prevented rivalry and ensured that each took his place not by human preference but by what seemed the appointment of God. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's service should be ordered fairly, with careful record ('Shemaiah the scribe wrote them'), so that everyone knows his duty and none is wronged. The chapter is teaching us that God is not the author of confusion but of order and peace (1 Cor 14:33), and that His worship is to be conducted with the same care for fairness, accountability, and reverence that David here shows in arranging the priests of the LORD's house.

Now the first lot came forth to Jehoiarib, the second to Jedaiah, The third to Harim, the fourth to Seorim, The fifth to Malchijah, the sixth to Mijamin, The seventh to Hakkoz, the eighth to Abijah, The ninth to Jeshua, the tenth to Shecaniah, The eleventh to Eliashib, the twelfth to Jakim, The thirteenth to Huppah, the fourteenth to Jeshebeab, The fifteenth to Bilgah, the sixteenth to Immer, The seventeenth to Hezir, the eighteenth to Aphses, The nineteenth to Pethahiah, the twentieth to Jehezekel, The one and twentieth to Jachin, the two and twentieth to Gamul, The three and twentieth to Delaiah, the four and twentieth to Maaziah. These were the orderings of them in their service to come into the house of the LORD, according to their manner, under Aaron their father, as the LORD God of Israel had commanded him. Guidance: By lot the priests are divided into twenty-four courses, each named and numbered, that they might serve in the house of the LORD in orderly rotation 'according to their manner... as the LORD God of Israel had commanded.' Each course would take its turn ministering at the temple, so that the worship of God went on continually and every priestly family had its appointed season of service. The chapter is showing us the wisdom of order and rotation in the work of God: rather than confusion or the crowding of some while others stood idle, each course knew exactly when it was to serve. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's worship is to be carried on continually and decently, with every servant taking his turn in his appointed place. The casting of the lot teaches us that the disposing of our place and portion is in God's hands — 'the lot is cast into the lap; but the whole disposing thereof is of the LORD' (Prov 16:33); each priest received his course as from God, and so could serve without envy or complaint. The chapter is teaching us to be content with the place and the season of service the LORD assigns us, whether we are called to minister first like Jehoiarib or last like Maaziah; the order is God's, and every turn is honourable. And there is a precious gospel note hidden here: the eighth course, Abijah, is the very course to which Zacharias belonged, the priest who, a thousand years later, was burning incense in his appointed week when the angel announced the birth of John the Baptist (Luke 1:5-13). The faithful keeping of these courses, century after century, meant that when the fullness of time came, a godly priest was at his post to receive the news that the forerunner of the Messiah would be born. The chapter is teaching us that God works His greatest purposes through the faithful keeping of ordinary, appointed duties; the orderly service David established here was still being kept when the gospel dawned. Let us serve faithfully in our appointed course, for we know not what God may do through our quiet, ordered obedience.

And the rest of the sons of Levi were these: Of the sons of Amram; Shubael: of the sons of Shubael; Jehdeiah. Concerning Rehabiah: of the sons of Rehabiah, the first was Isshiah. Of the Izharites; Shelomoth: of the sons of Shelomoth; Jahath. And the sons of Hebron; Jeriah the first, Amariah the second, Jahaziel the third, Jekameam the fourth. Of the sons of Uzziel; Michah: of the sons of Michah; Shamir. The brother of Michah was Isshiah: of the sons of Isshiah; Zechariah. The sons of Merari were Mahli and Mushi: the sons of Jaaziah; Beno. The sons of Merari by Jaaziah; Beno, and Shoham, and Zaccur, and Ibri. Of Mahli came Eleazar, who had no sons. Concerning Kish: the son of Kish was Jerahmeel. The sons also of Mushi; Mahli, and Eder, and Jerimoth. These were the sons of the Levites after the house of their fathers. These likewise cast lots over against their brethren the sons of Aaron in the presence of David the king, and Zadok, and Ahimelech, and the chief of the fathers of the priests and Levites, even the principal fathers over against their younger brethren. Guidance: The Chronicler completes the arrangement by recording the rest of the Levites — the families who would assist the priests — and notes that they too 'cast lots over against their brethren the sons of Aaron,' so that the Levites were assigned to serve alongside the priestly courses, the whole work of the house dovetailing together. Significantly, the lot was cast for the older and younger families alike: 'the principal fathers over against their younger brethren' — no precedence given to seniority, all subject equally to the lot. The chapter is showing us the comprehensive ordering of all who served God's house — not only the priests but the assisting Levites, each appointed his place, so that priest and Levite worked together in harmony. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's work requires many hands in many roles, and that all of them — the priests at the altar and the Levites who supported them — are needed and honoured. The chapter is teaching us, in the casting of lots for older and younger alike, the impartiality of God's appointments: the senior families had no automatic advantage over the younger, but all received their place by the lot, as from the LORD. There is a beautiful equality before God in this; whatever our age or standing, our place in His service is His to assign. The chapter is teaching us that the body of God's servants is to function together, each in his lot, supporting one another in the common work — as Paul would later teach that the church is one body with many members, each needful, each placed by God where it pleases Him (1 Cor 12). The chapter is teaching us to take our assigned place gladly, to serve in harmony with our brethren, and to trust the wisdom of God who orders the whole household of faith, giving to each his portion and his task for the good of the whole and the glory of His name.

These likewise cast lots over against their brethren the sons of Aaron in the presence of David the king, and Zadok, and Ahimelech, and the chief of the fathers of the priests and Levites, even the principal fathers over against their younger brethren. Guidance: The Chronicler completes the arrangement by recording the rest of the Levites — the families who would assist the priests — and notes that they too 'cast lots over against their brethren the sons of Aaron,' so that the Levites were assigned to serve alongside the priestly courses, the whole work of the house dovetailing together. Significantly, the lot was cast for the older and younger families alike: 'the principal fathers over against their younger brethren' — no precedence given to seniority, all subject equally to the lot. The chapter is showing us the comprehensive ordering of all who served God's house — not only the priests but the assisting Levites, each appointed his place, so that priest and Levite worked together in harmony. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's work requires many hands in many roles, and that all of them — the priests at the altar and the Levites who supported them — are needed and honoured. The chapter is teaching us, in the casting of lots for older and younger alike, the impartiality of God's appointments: the senior families had no automatic advantage over the younger, but all received their place by the lot, as from the LORD. There is a beautiful equality before God in this; whatever our age or standing, our place in His service is His to assign. The chapter is teaching us that the body of God's servants is to function together, each in his lot, supporting one another in the common work — as Paul would later teach that the church is one body with many members, each needful, each placed by God where it pleases Him (1 Cor 12). The chapter is teaching us to take our assigned place gladly, to serve in harmony with our brethren, and to trust the wisdom of God who orders the whole household of faith, giving to each his portion and his task for the good of the whole and the glory of His name.
